messages.content:
1	---
     2	name: creative-skill
     3	description: Develop creative direction from user intent — concept previews, creative scripts, subject element mapping. Use when the idea is vague, direction needs confirmation, or references need creative interpretation. Not for confirmed briefs (generation-skill) or fixing existing videos (modification-skill).
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Usage
     7	
     8	This skill **starts from the user's description, intelligently assesses video type intent, invokes the appropriate knowledge base, forms a creative direction, and presents it through visual content**; it decides whether to produce a concept preview for user confirmation, and — once direction is confirmed — **develops the full sequence-level creative script** (the complete story broken into sequences with shot outlines, style notes, and pacing) before handing off to generation-skill.
     9	
    10	**Input**:
    11	- **Scenario A (New)**: User description (text / voice / reference materials — may be incomplete).
    12	- **Scenario B (Revision)**: Existing creative direction + revision requests and supplementary information.
    13	
    14	**Output**:
    15	- **Phase 1 — Direction confirmation**:
    16	  - **Confirmed-brief override**: When the user already provides a production-ready brief — explicit shot structure, timing, subject/scene specification, and enough detail to execute without inventing a new concept — **skip concept previews entirely**. Do not force the user through a direction-confirmation stage just because the video is >10s. In this case, creative-skill's job is only to preserve the user's structure, fill minor production gaps, and hand off a faithful execution plan to generation-skill.
    17	  - **Preview trigger — use only when it reduces production risk**: Do **not** default to preview just because the video is >10s. Use a preview only when at least one of these is true: (a) the creative direction is still ambiguous, (b) the user needs to confirm aesthetics before full production, (c) recurring people/products must be checked for consistency, or (d) audio feeling / timbre / atmosphere needs a quick taste before committing. If the direction is already clear enough to execute, go straight to the full creative script.
    18	  - **Preferred preview format — lightweight first**: A preview can be **either** (1) **one strong still image / key frame** or (2) **one short video clip**. Do not assume preview must be video. Use a still image when the main question is visual beauty, art direction, product/person look, or broad narrative framing. Use a short clip only when motion, pacing, or sound feeling is materially important to the decision. When a still image is used, pair it with a concise textual note on the intended sound/timbre/atmosphere rather than forcing a low-confidence video.
    19	  - **If using video preview**: Keep it short and purpose-built — enough to judge the direction, not enough to over-commit the pipeline to an early Grok interpretation. The preview's job is to let the user confirm **visual aesthetics, rough storytelling, character/product consistency, and the feel of sound / voice / atmosphere**. **Do not use voiceover in previews.** Music is allowed only when the preview itself is being judged as a standalone tonal sample; otherwise keep it focused on picture + ambience + action-tied sound.
    20	  - **How many previews**: Default to **one** preview direction when the concept is mostly clear and you only need confirmation. Produce **two** substantively different directions only when the brief is still open-ended and the user would genuinely benefit from an A/B choice. If two directions are shown, each must differ in at least two of: setting, action type, emotional tone, visual style.
    21	  - **Preview is not the production source of truth**: Never let a preview silently overwrite the user's original brief. The brief remains the primary source of truth for story, subject identity, product details, and required deliverables. If a preview was made with a text-only model (especially Grok), do **not** use it as the sole production anchor for identity-critical work; supplement it with proper character/product reference images before generation.
    22	  - **Fast path for short / clear requests**: For ≤10s videos — and for any longer request that is already clear enough to execute — Phase 1 deliverable can be a **brief text summary of creative direction** presented to the user: core concept, narrative structure, visual style, audio atmosphere. The user must see and acknowledge this summary before generation begins. Get to generation fast — this summary is 2–4 sentences, not a planning document.
    23	  - **Important**: Duration/aspect ratio confirmation (handled at the system prompt level) is **not** creative direction confirmation. The user saying "适合" to "15s 9:16" only confirms the format. Creative direction (narrative concept, visual style, emotional angle) must still be presented as a separate Phase 1 deliverable before production begins. Do not conflate the two.
    24	  - Internal creative planning can be thorough, but user-facing deliverables should be primarily visual/auditory with text only as brief context.
    25	- **Phase 2 — Full creative script** (after user confirms direction): A complete sequence-level creative script that maps the entire story from start to finish. This is the definitive handoff artifact for generation-skill and must include:
    26	  - **Sequence breakdown**: How many sequences, what each one covers (narrative beat, emotional arc, key action).
    27	  - **Emotion & information density arc**: A sense of where each sequence sits on the emotional intensity and information density curves — this doesn't need to be a formal annotation, but the creative script should make it clear whether a given moment is a quiet breathing beat, an information-heavy walkthrough, an emotional peak, etc. These two dimensions inform shot language choices (see `editing-rhythm-design.md` Section 4).
    28	  - **Per-sequence shot outline**: Core shots, shot types, key visual moments, and the narrative purpose of each sequence. Shot language (framing, camera movement, duration, breathing room) should be shaped by the emotional and informational weight of the moment. **Duration budget**: **≤10s per sequence** (all scenarios — no distinction between with/without reference video). Each sequence's storyboard must fit within Kling's prompt character limit. **Packaging key frames**: When a sequence involves on-screen text (selling points, brand name, decorative typography), annotate what packaging key frame image is needed — text content, visual style, composition context, and when in the sequence the text should appear. This annotation tells generation-skill to prepare the key frame image before generating that sequence. **For any readable text, number, KPI, UI screen, or end card that must appear in-frame, explicitly note whether it is an environment-layer reference image (`packaging_ref` / `ui_ref`) or unsupported post text.**
    29	  - **Style & audio notes per sequence**: Visual style continuity and **speech / sound / music strategy** by scenario. Follow **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section III for the three voice types (dialogue, monologue, voiceover) and their handling per scenario. Key creative decisions for this phase: (a) with reference video → plan voiceover/music/SFX for post only, always extract Sound Reference Context; (b) single ≤10s → design **dialogue / monologue + SFX / ambience** in prompt, and allow music plus off-screen narration in-prompt **only when this exact one segment is the final user delivery** with no expected later assembly or extension; otherwise keep them in post; (c) multi-sequence → design SFX per sequence plus only **in-sequence-eligible** dialogue/monologue, while keeping voiceover/music as a separate full-timeline audio design layer for post-assembly. For multi-sequence, each sequence's SFX notes must reference the **SFX coherence plan's shared ambient foundation** and describe how this sequence's unique sounds layer on top of it — not independently define a completely different sound world.
    30	  - **Visual Style Convention** (multi-sequence only): Define the unified visual language for the entire video — 4 constants that every segment must share:
    31	    - **Rendering style** (photorealistic / cinematic / painterly / illustration / motion graphics)
    32	    - **Color palette** (warm/cool/neutral, dominant tones)
    33	    - **Lighting mood** (natural / studio / dramatic / soft / golden hour)
    34	    - **Texture quality** (organic film grain / clean digital / dreamy soft focus)
    35	
    36	    These constants become a **style preamble** that generation-skill prepends to every segment's prompt, preventing each segment from independently interpreting the visual style. When the creative story involves a deliberate style shift (e.g., reality → fantasy), annotate which segments deviate and why — generation-skill will use this to plan appropriate transitions. If all segments share one visual world (the common case), state the convention once and note "applies to all sequences."
    37	  - **BGM timing mode recommendation** (when BGM is part of the audio plan): Recommend one of three modes for generation-skill:
    38	    - **Video-first** (default for multi-sequence narrative): Generate video first, analyze rhythm, then compose BGM to match.
    39	    - **Music-first**: When the creative direction is driven by a musical arc, user-provided audio, or choreography — generate/analyze audio first, then generate video informed by the beat structure.
    40	    - **Independent**: When BGM is purely ambient background with no expected sync to visual beats — generate in parallel.
    41	
    42	    Base the recommendation on the creative story's audio-visual relationship. If the narrative has specific moments where music should hit visual events (scene changes, emotional peaks), recommend video-first. If the user provided a music track or the concept is rhythm-driven, recommend music-first.
    43	  - **SFX coherence plan** (multi-sequence only): Define a **shared ambient foundation** — a consistent base-layer SFX character that should persist across all segments (e.g., "warm organic room tone" or "soft outdoor nature ambience"). On top of this base, plan a **designed SFX arc** across segments — how the sound evolves through the narrative, not random per-segment choices. This plan feeds into generation-skill's per-segment SFX prompt writing to prevent cross-segment audio discontinuity.
    44	  - **Pacing plan**: How the overall rhythm flows across sequences (e.g., slow build → climax → resolution).
    45	  - This script is internal — show the user a brief summary ("here's the full plan"), not the raw production document. **User-facing summary filter** (mandatory before presenting): strip ALL internal production terms — replace with natural equivalents. Banned terms → replacements: "Seq 1/2/3" → "第一个/第二个/第三个场景"; "SFX co-gen" / "co-generated" / "co-generation" → "同步音效" or just describe the sounds; "post-assembly" / "后期合成" → "配乐" or omit; "parallel" / "serial" / "hybrid" → omit entirely; "sound on/off" → omit; model names (Grok, Kling, Seedream) → omit. The user should read a creative summary, not a production spec.
    46	  - **Production strategy recommendation**: For multi-sequence videos (>10s), the creative script must include a recommended production strategy — **parallel** or **serial** — based on the reference coverage assessment from Step 3 of Subject Element Decomposition. When all cross-sequence elements have visual references → recommend parallel (faster). When gaps exist → recommend serial or specify which gaps must be filled first to enable parallel. This recommendation is binding for generation-skill.
    47	    - **Continuous-action override**: Reference coverage alone does not determine the strategy. When adjacent sequences depict a **single character's unbroken motion arc** — no scene change, no time skip, the action flows directly from one sequence into the next (e.g., a character leaps off a rooftop in Seq 2 and lands on a staircase in Seq 3) — those sequences **must be recommended as serial**, regardless of reference coverage. Parallel generation of continuous action produces camera/pose discontinuities at the cut point that no amount of reference images can prevent. Independent scenes (different location, different time, or a clear narrative break) can be parallel. Mixed videos → recommend hybrid (serial for continuous blocks, parallel for independent scenes).
    48	
    49	**CRITICAL — Phase 2 is not optional**: When the user confirms a direction from concept previews, **Phase 2 must be completed before any generation begins**. Do not hand off to generation-skill with only the concept preview and the user's choice — generation-skill requires the full creative script, Subject Element Registry, and reference coverage verification. Skipping Phase 2 causes generation-skill to improvise the narrative structure, miss reference gaps, and produce inconsistent multi-segment videos.
    50	
    51	**Constraints & Behavior**:
    52	
    53	1. **Video Type Intent Assessment**: Based on user description, classify as narrative-driven creative ad / product showcase ad / short-form drama / tutorial / mood/atmosphere video, etc., and select the appropriate knowledge base accordingly. Maximize inference from available information and produce concept previews quickly. **Duration and aspect ratio are confirmed before this skill is invoked** (handled at the system prompt level) — do not re-ask for them. Never conduct questionnaire-style collection.
    54	   - **Confirmed brief detection — highest priority**: Before applying any preview rule, check whether the user has already provided a production-ready script. Treat the request as a **confirmed brief** when most of the following are present: (a) explicit shot/scene sequence, (b) timing per beat or clear total timing allocation, (c) subject/product/model description, (d) environment/style direction, and (e) explicit audio direction such as dialogue or voiceover copy. When this threshold is met, **do not generate concept previews** and do not reframe the request into alternative concepts. Preserve the user's structure, only fill execution gaps, and hand off directly to generation-skill.
    55	   - **Commercial intent activation**: When the creative brief carries commercial/conversion intent — the user wants the video to sell, promote, or drive action — the creative direction must reflect advertising thinking: persuasion and conversion, not pure aesthetic exhibition. An ad creative must answer: what hooks the viewer in the opening? what core benefit is communicated? what drives desire (demonstration, contrast, scenario identification)? The model already possesses advertising creative knowledge (AIDA, Problem→Solution, Before/After, etc.) — this rule ensures that knowledge is activated in commercial contexts rather than overridden by a default content/aesthetic approach.
    56	   - **Structured vs. vague input detection**: When the user provides a shot sequence (e.g., "产品图→拉远咖啡馆→顾客微笑" or numbered shots), treat these as **anchored structure** — the user's shots must appear in the final video as described. If the confirmed duration exceeds what the user's shots can fill, **do not silently expand into a different narrative**. Instead: (a) use the user's shots as anchor points and expand each into a fuller scene segment, or (b) briefly tell the user the expansion plan before proceeding (e.g., "你的3个镜头给了我骨架，我会把每个扩展成一段场景来填满30秒——产品特写8s + 咖啡馆10s + 微笑定格8s + 收尾4s，可以吗？"). This is the one case where a brief confirmation is appropriate — it's not a questionnaire, it's respecting the user's creative structure.
    57	2. **Creative Design & Knowledge Base Invocation**: Understand core needs and communication goals → extract narrative framework and visual style direction → invoke general knowledge bases by type (narrative structure, creative principles). Type-specific knowledge bases provide specialized methodologies: `narrative-ad-creative-kb.md` covers narrative-driven creative ads (15s+ story-form ads using the "Relationship Restructuring" methodology). For other ad types (product showcase, performance ads, short-form product demos), the model's own advertising creative knowledge is sufficient — no dedicated knowledge base needed. Reference shot design, editing rhythm, audio, **dialogue/monologue**, voiceover, visual packaging knowledge bases as needed. Style design follows the "qualitative" principle — provide direction, not execution parameters.
    58	3. **Narrative & Style Output**: Output core concept and narrative structure summary; at the style level, describe how visual / rhythm / sound layers serve the concept and narrative without entering execution-level detail. Present to users as brief text + visual content — do not output lengthy planning documents.
    59	4. **Intent Insight & Elevation**: Extract core emotional appeal from the request; use narrative models (e.g., three-act structure) to decompose into visual elements and elevate the concept.
    60	5. **Preview-Before-Production Decision**: The decision follows a **duration threshold**:
    61	   - **Override comes first**: If rule 1 classifies the request as a **confirmed brief**, skip this entire preview decision block and proceed directly to execution planning.
    62	   - **≤10s**: No concept preview. Invest in creative direction, then generate the full-length video directly — it serves as both the deliverable and the first creative test. If the direction doesn't land, iterate on the full video.
    63	   - **>10s**: Produce **two 5–10 second concept previews** with substantively different creative cores, capturing core mood, key moments, and reference-worthy elements. Gather feedback, then expand the chosen direction to full length.
    64	   - **Model routing for concept previews**:
    65	     - **No user assets** (pure text request): Use **Grok** — fast text-to-video with built-in audio.
    66	     - **User provided assets** (images, video, any reference material): Use **Kling** — create video with reference images and sound enabled.
    67	     - See **references/video-models-scheduling.md** for routing details.
    68	   - **Concept previews must be complete audio-visual impressions** — not silent video. Use sound-on single-segment previews built around **SFX / ambience** and, when the scene itself needs character speech, eligible **dialogue / monologue**. Music is optional and only appropriate when the preview is being shown as a standalone one-segment tonal sample. Off-screen narration is usually unnecessary in previews, but may be used when the preview itself is intentionally treated as a self-contained one-shot tonal sample for the user to judge. **Every concept preview prompt must include concrete SFX descriptions** — previews are the user's first impression of the video's "feel," and that feel includes sound. Describe at least one specific sound event tied to on-screen action (e.g., "SFX: liquid splash as the bottle tilts", "the click of heels on marble"). Do not submit a sound-on preview prompt without SFX — it wastes co-generation and delivers a flat audio experience. When communicating with users, never use jargon like "PV" — use natural phrases like "let me make a quick version for you to see" or "let's set the tone first."
    69	6. **User-Requested Storyboard / Key Frame Confirmation**: When the user explicitly asks to see storyboard panels or key frame images before video production ("先出分镜图"、"先给我看关键帧"、"生成几张分镜我确认"), this is a valid direction-confirmation flow that supplements or replaces concept preview videos.
    70	   - **Reference images are mandatory**: Every `image_generate` call for storyboard panels must pass the user's reference images as visual input — the same "reference images are the anchor, not prompt text" principle from generation-skill applies here. Do **not** substitute `analyze_file_content` text descriptions for actual image references. Text descriptions are useful for understanding what's in the image internally, but the image file itself must be passed to the generation model.
    71	   - **Multi-character scenes — all references in every call**: When the storyboard panel depicts multiple characters that have separate reference images, **all character references must be passed into the same `image_generate` call**. Use Gemini `image2image` mode with `image_urls` for multi-image input (see `references/image-generation-guide.md` Section II.6). Do not alternate which single reference to pass per panel — this produces frames where only one character matches its reference.
    72	   - **Consistency across panels**: Pass the same complete set of character/subject reference images to every storyboard panel generation call, not different subsets. Variation between panels should come from **prompt differences** (pose, interaction, scene), not from reference image selection.
    73	   - **Retry diagnosis**: When the user reports that storyboard characters "don't look like the reference" ("不像"、"和参考图不一样"), the first diagnostic step is: **were the reference images actually passed to the generation model?** Check whether the `image_generate` call included the user's images in the input parameters. If not, fix the reference pipeline — do not just switch models or rewrite the prompt description. Switching models without fixing the missing-reference root cause will repeat the same failure.
    74	   - **Aspect ratio alignment**: Generate storyboard panels in the same aspect ratio as the target video (e.g., 9:16 for vertical video) so the user evaluates compositions that match the final output framing.
    75	   - **Prompt hygiene for storyboard image generation**: Storyboard / key-frame prompts must stay purely visual. Do **not** write aspect ratios, pixel sizes, quality labels (`4K`, `HD`), hex/RGB color codes, internal IDs, or other technical metadata into the prompt text. Put aspect ratio / size only in the tool parameters per `references/image-generation-guide.md`. If prompt text contains a token that would look wrong if literally printed on the image, remove or rewrite it before calling `image_generate`.
    76	7. **Subject Element Decomposition**: Analyze the creative story and user assets to build a **Subject Element Registry** — a structured inventory of every distinct visual/audio subject that must appear in the final video and where it comes from.
    77	
    78	   **When to perform**: This step scales with the gap between what the user provides and what the video requires.
    79	   - **User assets already cover key elements** (e.g., user uploaded product photos, character images, environment references that together cover the main visual subjects): **Streamline** — do a quick mental inventory to confirm coverage, note any gaps briefly, and move on. No need to produce a formal structured registry. The user's assets speak for themselves.
    80	   - **User assets are sparse or absent** (e.g., pure text request, only one product photo but the story involves characters and environments): **Full decomposition required** — walk through all four steps below and produce a structured registry. The less the user provides, the more gaps exist, and the more important it is to identify and fill them before generation.
    81	   - **Rule of thumb**: If the creative story requires subject elements (characters, environments, brand elements) that have **no visual reference from any source** (user assets, concept preview key frames, or supplementary generation), those elements will drift across sequences during generation. The registry exists to catch this before it happens.
    82	   - **Do not let style reference masquerade as subject coverage**: A `style_ref` image or a single cinematic still can anchor **look** (lighting, color, texture, mood), but it does **not** count as full coverage for recurring characters, specific real-person analogs, relationship scenes, or reusable locations. If the story depends on the audience recognizing "this is the same person" or "this is the same room/company/world" across multiple sequences, you need dedicated `character_ref` / `environment_ref` assets — not just one attractive style frame.
    83	   - **Auto-supplement for complex scenes**: When the story involves characters in close-up or mid-shot (face visible), people interacting with objects (hands touching phones, passing items, operating devices), or specific UI/interface elements — these require visual references to generate reliably. Pure text-to-video cannot maintain face consistency, hand-object contact accuracy, or UI legibility. When the user hasn't provided these references, **proactively generate them** (character reference images, key frame compositions showing the interaction) before proceeding to video generation. This is not asking the user for more input — it's the agent doing its own prep work.
    84	   - **Multi-angle character references for action scenes**: When the creative story involves a character performing dynamic full-body actions (running, jumping, fighting, dancing) or being filmed from non-frontal angles (side, back, overhead), a single front-facing portrait is **insufficient** for maintaining character consistency — the model has no anchor for what the character looks like from other angles and will hallucinate those views, often producing deformed results. If the user provides only one angle, **proactively ask whether they have a character sheet** (multi-angle reference). If they don't, **generate one** using an image generation tool — a simple multi-angle turnaround (front, 3/4, side, back) of the character in neutral pose. This supplementary image becomes a critical reference asset for all sequences featuring dynamic action. Do not wait until character breakdown occurs in the output to address this — it is a preventive measure during creative planning.
    85	   - **Narrative real-person / multi-character scenes need explicit identity coverage**: When the story centers on a recognizable real person, a real-person analog ("Jobs-like founder", "Musk-like CEO"), or a recurring relationship between two or more named characters, do not rely on prompt text or one style still to carry identity. Generate dedicated reference assets for every recurring principal character before handoff. If a later sequence depends on a specific relationship beat (meeting, confrontation, handshake, interview, alliance), also prepare enough environment/context reference so the interaction does not collapse into generic stock imagery.
    86	
    87	   **Step 1 — Extract subject elements from the creative story**: Walk through the narrative and list every distinct subject that needs visual representation:
    88	   - **Product / hero object**: The primary item being featured (e.g., a handbag, a phone, a bottle). This is almost always the highest-priority reference element. **Physical scale matters**: note the product's real-world size relative to a person (palm-sized, handheld, tabletop, etc.). This scale relationship must be preserved throughout the reference chain — AI image models tend to exaggerate product size for visual prominence, which cascades into video generation if uncorrected. When generating supplementary reference images that include the product alongside a person, **always describe realistic scale** in the prompt (e.g., "a small palm-sized yogurt cup held naturally in one hand" rather than just "holding a yogurt cup").
    89	   - **Characters / models**: Each distinct person or character that appears. Note their role (protagonist, background, hands-only).
    90	   - **Environment / setting**: Key locations or backdrops (kitchen, city street, studio).
    91	   - **Brand elements**: Logo, packaging, typography, color scheme.
    92	   - **Props / secondary objects**: Items that interact with the product or characters (gift box, table setting, vehicle).
    93	   - **Style / mood anchors**: Visual tone, color palette, lighting direction, texture.
    94	
    95	   **Step 2 — Map user assets to subject elements**: For each uploaded asset, determine **what subject element it represents**, not just what it "looks like." This requires cross-referencing across all uploads:
    96	   - If the user uploads 3 images that all contain the same product but with different models → the **product** is the common reference subject; the models are context, not the reference target (unless the user explicitly says "use this model").
    97	   - If the user uploads a mood board image → it's a **style/mood anchor**, not a character or product reference.
    98	   - If the user uploads a video → determine whether it's a **motion/style reference** (borrow camera work, pacing) or a **subject reference** (the character/product in the video matters).
    99	   - **Assign each asset a clear role label**: `product_ref`, `character_ref`, `style_ref`, `environment_ref`, `motion_ref`, `packaging_ref`. One asset can serve multiple roles (e.g., a product photo that also establishes the color palette).
   100
messages.name: read_file

messages.content:
The provided video is a classic example of a **whiteboard animation**, designed to be engaging, educational, and professionally persuasive. Here is a detailed breakdown of its visual and narrative style:

### **Visual Style**

*   **Hand-Drawn Whiteboard Aesthetic:** The central visual conceit is a human hand holding a black marker, appearing to draw the illustrations in real-time on a clean, stark white background. This creates a sense of "live" creation that holds the viewer’s attention better than static slides.
*   **Color Palette:** The palette is highly restricted and purposeful. It primarily uses **black** for outlines and text, **white** for the background, and a **vibrant red-to-orange gradient** for branding, emphasis, and "filling in" key elements. This high contrast ensures that the most important information (the red elements) pops immediately.
*   **Iconography and Symbolism:** The video relies on universal business and tech symbols:
    *   **Lightbulbs** for innovation.
    *   **Clouds and ladders** for cloud computing and potential.
    *   **Gears and circuits** for automation and technology.
    *   **Bar charts and buildings** for growth and corporate scale.
    *   **Puzzle pieces** for co-creation and partnership.
*   **Character Design:** The people depicted are stylized and minimalist. They often lack detailed facial features, representing "the professional" or "the team" rather than specific individuals. They are drawn with clean lines and occasional red accents (like a red shirt or tie) to tie them into the brand identity.
*   **Typography:** The text is clean, bold, and modern. The brand name "WNS" is prominent, and labels for various services (e.g., "WNS TRAC," "Domain expertise") use a simple sans-serif font that mirrors the clarity of the drawings.
*   **Animation Pacing and Transitions:** 
    *   **Synchronized Drawing:** The marker moves in sync with the voiceover, "completing" a drawing just as the speaker finishes a point.
    *   **Camera Movement:** The "camera" frequently pans across the white canvas to reveal new sections, creating a sense of a continuous, expanding journey rather than separate, disconnected slides.
    *   **Erase and Replace:** To change scenes, the screen often wipes clean or the hand draws over previous elements to transform them.

### **Narrative and Story Structure**

*   **The "What If" Opening (The Hook):** The story begins with a series of rhetorical "What if?" questions. This structure identifies the viewer’s pain points and aspirations (driving transformation, leveraging cloud tech, being a leader) before offering a solution.
*   **The Introduction (The Solution):** At the 0:24 mark, the brand "WNS" is introduced as the answer to the previously posed questions.
*   **Proof of Concept (The History):** The narrative moves into a "growth" phase, using a timeline (2002–2022) and rising skyscrapers to visually represent the company's longevity, stability, and expanding global footprint.
*   **Feature Breakdown (The Details):** The video then categorizes WNS’s specific offerings. It uses a "reveal" method where icons and names (like WNS IP labs) appear one by one, allowing the viewer to digest each piece of information without being overwhelmed.
*   **Core Philosophy (The Conclusion):** The story concludes with the concept of "Co-creation." The visual of hands putting puzzle pieces together provides a warm, collaborative ending, shifting from "what we do" to "how we work with you."
*   **Pacing Rhythm:** The rhythm is brisk but steady. Each "scene" or thought lasts roughly 5–10 seconds, maintaining a momentum that prevents the viewer from losing interest while ensuring the educational content is clear.

messages.content:
1	---
     2	name: generation-skill
     3	description: Execute full video production from a confirmed creative brief. Handles sequence planning, model routing (Seedance / Kling / Grok — four-layer system), generation, and post-assembly audio (BGM + off-screen narration only). Use when creative direction is confirmed and production is needed. Not for vague ideas (creative-skill) or modifying existing videos (modification-skill).
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Usage
     7	
     8	**Input**: A locked creative brief (or the creative script produced by creative-skill), including the **recommended production model** (Seedance / Seedance Fast / Kling / Grok — determined by creative-skill during Phase 2), optionally a **Subject Element Registry**, optionally a **concept preview** (with extracted key frames), supplementary reference assets, and optional reference image/video/audio from the user. **Note on images**: All images are plain references — no first-frame/end-frame distinction at the API level; describe first-frame intent in the prompt. **Audio strategy**: Audio co-generation is the #1 priority — see **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Sections III and VI. Post-assembly is limited to BGM and off-screen narration for multi-sequence workflows. Do not use post-production for SFX or on-screen dialogue.
     9	
    10	**PREREQUISITE CHECK**: Before starting any generation, verify that a complete creative script exists with sequence breakdown and per-sequence shot outlines. If only a concept preview selection exists without a full script (e.g., user just said "direction A"), **do not proceed** — return to creative-skill to develop the full Phase 2 script first. Generating sequences without a unified narrative plan produces disjointed, inconsistent video.
    11	
    12	**STORYBOARD CONFIRMATION PROTOCOL** (applies when generation-skill operates directly from user brief — no locked creative script from creative-skill):
    13	
    14	When the user provides a brief with reference images and format confirmation, and no creative-skill script exists, follow this lightweight confirmation protocol before generating video:
    15	
    16	1. **Present the sequence plan as text** — include the Scene Reference → Sequence Mapping table (Rule 2) and a one-line description per sequence. Ask if the direction is OK. **Do not generate storyboard images by default** — text descriptions are sufficient for direction alignment. Only generate storyboard key frames if the user explicitly requests visual previews.
    17	
    18	2. **If storyboard images are generated**: Collect feedback. The user may confirm the direction, request scene changes, or flag character appearance issues. **Separate scene direction from character appearance**: if the user's feedback is solely about the character (not the scenes), fix the character reference image once and proceed to video generation — do not regenerate all storyboard frames.
    19	
    20	3. **Maximum 2 confirmation rounds**: After the user has confirmed the scene direction (even implicitly — e.g., feedback is only about character details, not scene content), proceed to video generation. Minor character adjustments (remove accessories, clean up details) should be resolved in a single character reference fix, not through additional storyboard rounds.
    21	
    22	4. **Anti-loop detection**: If 2+ consecutive user messages address only character appearance and not scene direction, the scene direction is implicitly confirmed. Consolidate all character feedback into one reference image fix, then immediately start video generation.
    23	
    24	**Output**: The assembled finished video, along with a brief note on the strategy used — expand only when the user asks or an error occurs. **Never expose internal production terms** (sequence numbers, generation modes, sound on/off, model names) to the user — use natural creative language.
    25	
    26	**Constraints & Behavior**:
    27	
    28	1. **Model selection**: Execute using the model recommended by creative-skill in the handoff. Creative-skill determines the production model based on the four-layer routing system (see **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section I):
    29	   - **L1 Grok** (`fal` / `xai/grok-imagine-video/text-to-video`): Non-final pure T2V previews without precision needs, or NSFW content.
    30	   - **L2 Seedance Fast** (`seedance` / `doubao-seedance-2-0-fast-260128`): Quality previews, default pure T2V.
    31	   - **L3 Seedance** (`seedance` / `doubao-seedance-2-0-260128`): Production default — text-driven, multimodal mixed, long narrative.
    32	   - **L4 Kling** (`kling` / `kling-v3-omni`): Real human faces, copyrighted IP, readable in-frame text/UI, or selective micro-detail cases.
    33	   - **User intent overrides**: User explicitly wants speed → Grok; user explicitly wants quality → Seedance.
    34	   - Generation-skill only overrides the model on degradation (see Rule 19 Fallback). When the selected model fails, follow the degradation chain while preserving audio co-generation and reference consistency.
    35	   - **Pre-call model verification** (mandatory for every `video_generate` call): Before submitting the call, verify that `provider`, `model`, `mode`, and the `provider_param` key **all match** the routing decision. Use the definitive mapping table in **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section I. Common error: the routing decision selects Seedance but the tool call is constructed with `provider: "kling"` + `kling_reference2video` param key — this is a plan-execution mismatch. All four fields must be consistent:
    36	
    37	     | Routing decision | `provider` | `model` | `mode` | `provider_param` key |
    38	     |-----------------|-----------|---------|--------|---------------------|
    39	     | Seedance | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    40	     | Seedance Fast | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-fast-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    41	     | Kling | `kling` | `kling-v3-omni` | `reference2video` | `kling_reference2video` |
    42	     | Grok | `fal` | `xai/grok-imagine-video/text-to-video` | `text2video` | `fal_grok_text2video` |
    43	
    44	   - **Reference-discard check**: If user-uploaded reference materials (images, video, audio) exist and the call uses `mode: "text2video"`, STOP — `text2video` cannot accept `image_list`/`video_list`/`audio_list`, so all references will be silently discarded. Re-route to Seedance or Kling with `reference2video` and include references in the call.
    45	   - **Image-list population check**: When user-uploaded images exist and the production model supports references (Seedance, Kling), `image_list` MUST contain at least the user's primary reference image. An empty `image_list` with available user images is a plan-execution mismatch.
    46	2. **Build the Production Blueprint**: Break the brief into an executable **Sequence** list. When **Grok** is selected, the plan is a single sequence; write one flowing narrative prompt per Grok's style. Each sequence represents a **self-contained narrative paragraph containing multiple shots** (see Section V in **references/video-models-scheduling.md**). Duration per sequence depends on the production model: **Seedance ≤15s**, **Kling ≤10s**, **Grok ≤10s**. **Single-call preference** (see **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section IV): When total video duration fits within the model's single-call max, strongly prefer generating the entire video in one call — use prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`, `[10–15s]`) to structure beats within a single prompt. This avoids unnecessary cross-segment consistency risk, redundant API calls, and post-assembly overhead. Split only when there is a clear production reason (independent scenes needing different references, isolated retry needs). For multi-sequence videos, leverage Seedance's 15s cap to reduce sequence count and cross-sequence consistency risk — let narrative rhythm determine natural break points within the model's limit. Plan all sequences upfront before generating any — map the full narrative arc first, then write individual prompts. End at a natural pause point so sequences can chain cleanly.
    47	   - **Shot duration heuristics** — when the sequence includes voiceover or on-screen text:
    48	     - **VO/text coverage**: Budget shot duration ≥ spoken/read duration + 0.5–1s slack (~3–4 Chinese chars/sec, ~2.5–3 English words/sec). When planning a sequence that hosts voiceover/narration, budget the sequence duration to be **at least** the estimated VO read time + 1s — never shorter. Example: a 20-character Chinese voiceover at 3–4 chars/sec needs 5–7s of speaking time; do not assign it to a 4s sequence. If the narrative structure constrains the sequence to a shorter duration, shorten the VO text to fit the available time.
    49	     - **Breathing room**: ~0.5–1s between narrated shots, ~1.5–2s visual-only between narrated sequences.
    50	     - **Shots without narration** follow emotional weight rather than text timing — see `editing-rhythm-design.md` Section 4.
    51	     - Sub-second precision is below model resolution — use timing cues as pacing guidance.
    52	   - **Visual Intent Pass** (multi-sequence only): Before writing any individual prompt, establish a unified visual language and produce a per-sequence design table.
    53	
    54	     **Step 1 — Visual Style Convention** (declare ONCE, applies to ALL segments): Define 4 constants that form the video's visual identity. Every segment must stay within this convention unless a segment has explicit narrative justification for deviation (which must be documented in the table's Narrative Justification cell).
    55	
    56	     | Constant | Example | What it controls |
    57	     |----------|---------|-----------------|
    58	     | **Rendering style** | photorealistic / cinematic / painterly / illustration / motion graphics | The fundamental look — mixing styles across segments (e.g., nature macro → CGI → studio) is the #1 cause of visual disconnection |
    59	     | **Color palette** | warm golden tones / cool desaturated / neutral with jade accents | Dominant color character — shifts of >2 stops between adjacent segments look like different videos |
    60	     | **Lighting mood** | soft natural / dramatic rim light / studio even / golden hour | Lighting consistency is as important as color — a studio-lit segment adjacent to a natural-light segment breaks immersion |
    61	     | **Texture quality** | organic film grain / clean digital / dreamy soft focus | Surface treatment — gritty next to polished reads as a style error, not variety |
    62	
    63	     These 4 constants become a **style preamble** that is prepended to every segment's prompt. Example: "Cinematic photorealistic look, warm golden palette with jade accents, soft diffused natural lighting, organic film-like texture." This preamble is not optional — it is the mechanism that prevents each segment from independently interpreting the visual style.
    64	
    65	     **Step 2 — Visual Intent Table** — one row per sequence:
    66	
    67	     | Seq | Color Temperature | Shot Anchor | Human Relationship | Transition In | Narrative Justification |
    68	     |-----|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|-------------------------|
    69	     | ... | ...               | ...         | ...                | ...           | ...                     |
    70	
    71	     Rules for filling the table:
    72	     - Every cell must contain an explicit absolute description (e.g., "cool overcast grey", "wide → mid-shot", "two workers in conversation"). No cell may be left blank or marked "same as above."
    73	     - Adjacent rows may share the same value when the narrative calls for continuity — but the justification cell must explain why (e.g., "maintains emotional momentum from seq2"). Similarity by default, not by intent, is what this step prevents.
    74	     - Transitions: carry meaning beyond cut/no-cut — note the reason (e.g., "dissolve — 20-year time jump", "cut — same scene, momentum continuity").
    75	     - **Color Temperature** values must be consistent with the Style Convention's color palette. If a segment needs a different temperature (e.g., flashback in cool blue), the Narrative Justification must explain the shift, and the transition must signal it.
    76	     - This table is produced once before any generation call. Because every row is self-contained with absolute values, it works identically for both serial and parallel generation.
    77	
    78	     **Step 3 — Style Drift Check** (validate before writing prompts): Scan the completed table row by row. For each adjacent pair of segments, verify:
    79	     - Would the two segments look like they belong in the same video? If segment A describes "macro nature photography in soft morning light" and segment B describes "CGI particle convergence in dark studio lighting," they will look like clips from two different projects.
    80	     - If a style shift is intentional (e.g., reality → fantasy transition), the Transition In cell must contain a transition type that signals the shift (dissolve, whiteout, motivated cut) and the Narrative Justification must explain why.
    81	     - If a style shift is unintentional — the segments just ended up with different visual languages because they describe different subjects — **unify them** by rewriting both to share the Style Convention's constants. Different subjects can coexist within one visual language; different visual languages cannot.
    82	
    83	     Prompts are written by translating each row's design decisions into language — not by re-inventing the visual design during prompt writing. **Every prompt starts with the Style Convention preamble**, followed by the segment-specific direction from the table row.
    84	   - **Scene Reference Image Coverage** (mandatory when user provides multiple distinct scene/environment reference images):
    85	     Count the number of distinct scene/environment reference images the user provided (different locations, backgrounds, or settings — not multiple angles of the same scene). The Production Blueprint must **use every user-provided scene reference image** — no image may be silently dropped.
    86	     - **Mapping is flexible**: N scene reference images do NOT require N sequences. A single ≤10s sequence can contain multiple scene transitions — pass multiple scene reference images into one sequence's `image_list` and describe the scene cuts in the prompt. Kling handles intelligent scene-splitting from text. Example: 3 scene references in a 10s sequence → prompt describes a 3-location montage, image_list contains all 3 scene images plus the character reference.
    87	     - **Sequence count is driven by duration and narrative, not reference count**: Plan sequences based on `target_duration / max_sequence_duration` and narrative arc, then distribute scene references across sequences. A 15s video with 4 scene references could be 2 sequences × ~8s (each covering 2 scenes) or 4 sequences × ~4s — the right split depends on pacing and transition design.
    88	     - **Explicit mapping table** (include in plan output to user):
    89	       | Seq | Scene References Used | Planned Duration |
    90	       |-----|----------------------|-----------------|
    91	       | 1   | Image 1 (street), Image 2 (station) | 8s |
    92	       | 2   | Image 3 (bridge), Image 4 (temple) | 8s |
    93	     - If any user-provided scene reference image is not mapped to a sequence, explain why in the plan.
    94	     - This rule supplements, not replaces, the Visual Intent Table — the mapping table is a planning tool; the Visual Intent Table governs visual consistency.
    95	   - **Speed-change segments**: When the creative brief calls for slow-motion or fast-motion within a segment, generate source material at the correct pre-speed duration: source duration = output duration × playback speed. Example: 5s of 0.5x slow-motion needs only 2.5s of source → generate with duration=3 (round up), then apply speed=0.5 in edit_spec using the full source (in=0, out=all frames). Do not generate full-length source and truncate with `out` to fit the timing — truncation discards the tail of the action, often the most important moment.
    96	   - **Voice Strategy Declaration** (mandatory before generation — any video with spoken audio): Before writing any generation prompt, classify every spoken audio element in the creative brief and assign it to exactly one source. This declaration is binding — once a line is assigned to a source, it cannot also be produced by the other source.
    97	     - **Step 1 — Classify each spoken element** into one of: **on-screen dialogue** (character speaks with visible lip movement), **off-screen narration** (voice accompanies visuals, no on-screen lip movement), **inner monologue** (character's internal thoughts, no lip movement), or **SFX/atmosphere** (non-speech sound tied to visual action). **Quick classification anchors for common request patterns**: Keywords signaling on-screen dialogue — "口播" (presenter/talking-head), "主播" (host), "出镜讲解" / "出镜介绍" (on-camera explainer), "AI讲解员" (AI presenter) → classify as on-screen dialogue, co-gen path only; do NOT call `audio_produce` for the same lines. Keywords signaling off-screen narration — "旁白" (voiceover/narration), "画外音" (off-screen voice), "旁白介绍" / "解说词" (narration copy) → classify as off-screen narration, TTS path only. When the request contains both (e.g., on-camera host plus a separate narrator), use the Mixed path — co-gen for on-screen lines, `audio_produce` for off-screen narration only.
    98	     - **Step 2 — Determine the voice routing** based on on-screen visibility and whether voice identity must be preserved across languages:
    99	       - **On-screen dialogue / monologue** (character visibly speaks — any visual style including 2D animation, illustration, motion graphics, realistic): **Co-generate path.** Include the dialogue/monologue in the sequence prompt (`sound: on`). Co-generation produces lip-synced, emotionally matched voice that is synchronized with the character's visible mouth movements — this cannot be replicated by post-assembly TTS regardless of visual style. Modern video models handle audio-visual co-generation well across all visual styles.
   100	       - **Off-screen narration / voiceover** (no on-screen speaker): **TTS path.** `sound: on` for SFX only in the prompt (no speech). Narration via `audio_produce` in post-assembly. This gives full voice control (consistent voice ID, pacing, tone) for narration that spans multiple sequences.

content.text:
Great — I've analyzed the reference thoroughly. The original is ~142 seconds at 1920×1080, 16:9. For a whiteboard explainer recreating this style with your HR automation story, I'd suggest **90 seconds at 16:9** — enough room for the full problem→solution arc without padding. That's our system cap zone and matches the educational rhythm of the reference perfectly.

Before I commit to generation, let me show you the visual direction first with a style frame — so you can see exactly how the whiteboard aesthetic and HR story will look before any video is made.

**Does 90 seconds, 16:9 work for you?**

While you confirm, here's what I'm planning visually — let me generate a style frame to show you the hand-drawn language, color accent logic, and scene staging I have in mind for this HR explainer.

Pure white background. A hand holding a black marker is in the corner mid-drawing. The scene shows: on the left side, a sketch-drawn frustrated stick figure sitting at a desk buried under stacks of hand-drawn paper documents with labels like 'ONBOARDING FORM', 'APPROVAL PENDING', 'MISSING DOCS' — all drawn in rough sketch outlines. Red-orange accent color highlights the chaotic pile of papers. An arrow points right to a simple hand-drawn flow diagram showing boxes connected by arrows: 'Employee Joins' → 'Documents Collected' → 'Approval Routed' → 'Compliance Checked' with a checkmark at the end, drawn in clean black sketch lines with teal/green accent fills on the final step. Bold hand-lettered title text at the top reads 'HR WORKFLOW AUTOMATION'. The overall style is classic whiteboard explainer: rough marker strokes, sketch-like imperfect outlines, limited color palette of black outlines plus red-orange and teal accents only, no gradients, no photos, no polish — pure educational hand-drawn illustration language.","size":"2k"}}}

skills_metadata.name: creative-skill
skills_metadata.description: Develop creative direction from user intent — concept previews, creative scripts, subject element mapping. Use when the idea is vague, direction needs confirmation, or references need creative interpretation. Not for confirmed briefs (generation-skill) or fixing existing videos (modification-skill).

skills_metadata.name: generation-skill
skills_metadata.description: Execute full video production from a confirmed creative brief. Handles sequence planning, model routing (Seedance / Kling / Grok — four-layer system), generation, and post-assembly audio (BGM + off-screen narration only). Use when creative direction is confirmed and production is needed. Not for vague ideas (creative-skill) or modifying existing videos (modification-skill).

skills_metadata.name: modification-skill
skills_metadata.description: Modify an existing produced video based on user feedback — re-generate (Seedance preferred, Kling for real-face/IP/fine-detail), visual-edit, or post-production adjust specific parts, then reassemble with audio. Use when the user wants changes to an existing video. Not for first-time creation (creative/generation-skill) or concept preview redos (creative-skill).

messages.content: Recreate the provided whiteboard explainer reference style for a new enterprise HR workflow automation service. Match the reference hand-drawn illustration language, whiteboard pacing, step-by-step explanation rhythm, sketch-like outlines, limited accent colors, and instructional scene progression. Replace the original content with this business story: growing companies struggle with employee onboarding, document collection, approval routing, and compliance tracking; then show how the platform streamlines each step with a clearer workflow, fewer manual follow-ups, and stronger visibility for HR teams. Keep the result simple, educational, and business-friendly. The final video should clearly feel like a whiteboard / hand-drawn explainer with visible sketch logic and guided explanation flow, not a polished motion graphics promo. <original-video>/workspace/uploaded/a_bzYsoG_Top Whiteboard Animation Video - Leading Tech Services_ Explainer Video - Motion_1.mp4</original-video>
